source_paper_title: Optimal Budget Aggregation with Star-Shaped Preference Domains
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brandt_2025, title={Optimal Budget Aggregation with Star-Shaped Preference Domains}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0723}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0723}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brandt, Felix and Greger, Matthias and Segal-Halevi, Erel and Suksompong, Warut}, year={2025}, month=dec }
context:
Section 8 (Conclusion), page 19

It would be interesting to identify other sensible utility models for which the Nash product rule is a most attractive aggregation mechanism, and to pinpoint domain conditions that cause impossibilities similar to Theorems 1 and 2. Some concrete open questions are:
• Does Theorem 3 also hold when weakening group-strategyproofness to strategyproofness? Is continuity required for the characterization of the Nash product rule?
title: Determine Nash product rule characterization without continuity or group-strategyproofness assumptions
background_latex:
Let there be a set of agents \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a set of alternatives \(M=\{1,\dots,m\}\) with \(m\ge 2\). The feasible outcomes are distributions \(q=(q_1,\dots,q_m)\) in the simplex \(\Delta^m:=\{q\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}: \sum_{j\in M} q_j=1\}\).

Each agent \(i\) has an ideal distribution (peak) \(p_i=(p_{i,1},\dots,p_{i,m})\in\Delta^m\). Under Leontief (ratio) utilities, agent \(i\)'s utility for outcome \(q\) is
\[
 u_i(q):=\min_{j\in M_i} \frac{q_j}{p_{i,j}},\qquad M_i:=\{j\in M: p_{i,j}>0\}.
\]
Thus \(u_i(q)\in[0,1]\), with \(u_i(q)=1\) iff \(q=p_i\), and \(u_i(q)=0\) iff \(q_j=0\) for some \(j\in M_i\).

A (tops-only) aggregation mechanism is a function \(f:(\Delta^m)^n\to \Delta^m\) mapping a profile \(P=(p_1,\dots,p_n)\) of reported peaks to an outcome \(f(P)\).

Core-type fairness (core fair share) is defined via blocking coalitions. A coalition \(S\subseteq N\) blocks an outcome \(q\in\Delta^m\) if there exists \(q'\in\Delta^m\) such that for every \(q''\in\Delta^m\),
\[
 u_i\Bigl(\tfrac{|S|}{n}q' + \bigl(1-\tfrac{|S|}{n}\bigr) q''\Bigr) > u_i(q)\quad\text{for all } i\in S.
\]
An outcome \(q\) satisfies core fair share if it admits no blocking coalition; a mechanism satisfies core fair share if it always outputs such an outcome.

Strategyproofness means that no single agent can benefit by misreporting her peak: for every profile \(P\), every agent \(i\), and every alternative report \(p_i'\in\Delta^m\), writing \(P'=(p_i',P_{-i})\), it holds that \(u_i(f(P))\ge u_i(f(P'))\) where \(u_i\) is the utility induced by the true peak \(p_i\). Group-strategyproofness strengthens this to require that no coalition can misreport so that every member weakly benefits and at least one strictly benefits.

The Nash product rule (Nash welfare maximization) for Leontief utilities selects
\[
\mathrm{NASH}(P)\in \arg\max_{q\in\Delta^m} \prod_{i\in N} u_i(q).
\]
For Leontief utilities, this maximizer is unique.

Continuity of a mechanism \(f\) means that small changes in the reported peaks (in any norm on the compact domain \((\Delta^m)^n\)) lead to small changes in the outcome \(f(P)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the Nash product rule \(\mathrm{NASH}:(\Delta^m)^n\to\Delta^m\) is uniquely characterized among all mechanisms \(f:(\Delta^m)^n\to\Delta^m\) for Leontief utilities by the axioms of core fair share and incentive compatibility, without requiring the full set of axioms used in the paper.

In particular, answer one or both of the following.

1) If \(f\) satisfies core fair share and (individual) strategyproofness for Leontief utilities, must \(f(P)=\mathrm{NASH}(P)\) for all profiles \(P\in(\Delta^m)^n\)?

2) If \(f\) satisfies core fair share and group-strategyproofness for Leontief utilities, is it necessary to additionally assume continuity in order to conclude \(f(P)=\mathrm{NASH}(P)\) for all \(P\)? Equivalently, does core fair share plus group-strategyproofness already force \(f\equiv \mathrm{NASH}\) even for possibly discontinuous mechanisms?

2) If \(f\) satisfies core fair share and group-strategyproofness for Leontief utilities, is it necessary to additionally assume continuity in order to conclude \(f(P)=\mathrm{NASH}(P)\) for all \(P\)? open_problem_quote:
It would be interesting to identify other sensible utility models for which the Nash product rule is a most attractive aggregation mechanism, and to pinpoint domain conditions that cause impossibilities similar to Theorems 1 and 2. Some concrete open questions are:
• Does Theorem 3 also hold when weakening group-strategyproofness to strategyproofness? Is continuity required for the characterization of the Nash product rule?
subject_classification: Social choice and mechanism design for budget aggregation
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample (if it exists) would be a discontinuous or merely strategyproof (not group-strategyproof) mechanism satisfying core fair share yet differing from \(\mathrm{NASH}\). Such objects can sometimes be constructed by piecewise definitions, but verifying core fair share involves quantifying over coalitions and redistributions, which is nontrivial to automate at scale except for very small \(n,m\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate mechanisms can be represented programmatically and tested on finite grids of profiles for small \(n,m\), providing a partial fitness signal (violations of strategyproofness/core constraints). However, conclusively proving the axioms for all profiles is difficult to automate, so evolutionary search would at best suggest conjectures or find small counterexamples rather than deliver a proof.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
